question: why does compost steam
title: Why Does Compost Steam? What That Rising Vapor Tells You About Your Pile
metaDescription: Steam rising from a compost pile is one of the best signs your pile is healthy and hot. Scotty explains the thermophilic biology behind it.
content:
Hey everybody. Walk out on a cool morning, stick a pitchfork in your pile, and you might see steam pour out like you cracked open a furnace. That's one of the best things that can happen to a composter. I promise you, it'll make you feel like a soil scientist.

That steam is the visible signature of thermophilic bacteria consuming organic matter at high heat. It's not rot. It's microbial biology at a full sprint.

## Heat Is a Byproduct of Biology

Here's what's happening inside your pile. When you build a proper hot compost, right ratio of carbon browns to nitrogen greens, good moisture, enough mass to self-insulate, you create conditions where aerobic bacteria colonize and multiply fast.

Mesophilic bacteria kick things off first. They like moderate temperatures, around 60 to 100°F. They hit the sugars and starches, and as they work, they generate heat as a metabolic byproduct. They eat, they reproduce, they exhale heat.

Once the pile climbs above 104°F, thermophilic bacteria take over. These organisms prefer 104 to 160°F. They tackle proteins, fats, and complex carbohydrates, the stuff that cold compost takes months to process, in a matter of days. The Bacillus genus dominates this phase, along with heat-loving fungi and actinomycetes.

All that microbial metabolism generates intense heat. Your pile becomes a biological furnace.

## Where the Steam Actually Comes From

Steam forms when heat causes moisture in your pile to evaporate. You're seeing hot, water-vapor-rich air escape into cooler ambient air. It condenses the same way your breath does on a cold morning.

That moisture was always there, you put it in when you watered, or it was already in your green materials. Your bacteria need that moisture to function. But once the pile climbs toward 140, 150, even 160°F, that moisture volatilizes. It becomes vapor. It rises.

When you turn the pile and expose the interior, you release that trapped, superheated moisture all at once. That's your steam moment. Dramatic, satisfying, completely biological.

The steam doesn't smell bad. It smells like warm, wet earth. Thermophilic bacteria working aerobically don't produce hydrogen sulfide or ammonia in any meaningful quantity. They produce CO₂, water vapor, and heat. That's it.

## Why a Cold Pile Is a Problem

If your pile isn't steaming, it isn't hot. And if it isn't hot, the thermophilic phase either hasn't kicked in or it stalled.

The most common reason a pile goes cold is oxygen depletion. Thermophilic bacteria are aerobic, they need oxygen. As they work, they use it up. When oxygen runs low, their activity slows. The pile cools. The steam stops.

That's why turning matters so much in hot composting. Every time you turn your pile, you're reintroducing oxygen to the system. You're restarting the engine. Within a day or two of a good turning, a properly built pile will heat back up and start steaming again.

A pile can also cool if it gets too wet, too dry, or if the carbon-to-nitrogen ratio gets thrown off. Gabe Brown writes in *Dirt to Soil* about how the biology in your soil, and your compost, is always seeking balance. Your job is to maintain conditions, not do the work yourself.

## The Pathogen-Killing Bonus

Steam also means something important: pathogen kill. When your pile reaches and holds above 131°F for several consecutive days, it kills weed seeds, E. coli, Salmonella, and plant disease organisms.

This is why hot composting beats cold composting. A cold pile will eventually produce compost, but it may still harbor weed seeds and pathogens. A properly hot pile that gets turned regularly, so all the material passes through the hot core, produces a sanitized, safe, finished product.

This was central to Albert Howard's work. He argued that the decay cycle, properly managed, not only returns nutrients to your soil but breaks the disease cycle. Nothing survives a well-managed hot pile.

## What to Do When the Steam Stops

When your pile cools and stops steaming, the fix is almost always one of these: turn it for oxygen, water it for moisture, or add a fresh nitrogen source like food scraps or fresh green clippings to reinvigorate the bacterial population.

Step back and wait 24 to 48 hours. If the pile was properly built, the steam will come back. You'll see it on that cool morning and remember why you got into composting in the first place.

Steam means your pile is alive. Billions of thermophilic bacteria are doing the most important work in your garden, converting raw organic matter into stable humus that feeds plants, holds water, and builds the kind of soil that keeps giving year after year.

That's not garbage decomposing. That's the decay cycle turning waste into wealth.

## Sources

- [Cornell Composting, Compost Microorganisms](https://compost.css.cornell.edu/microorg.html)
- [Thermophilic Bacteria and Their Thermozymes in Composting, Springer Nature](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40538-023-00381-z)
- [Why Does My Compost Get Hot?, The Rot](https://therot.substack.com/p/why-does-my-compost-get-hot)
- [Thermophilic Composting, Permaculture Design Course Handbook](https://treeyopermacultureedu.com/chapter-8-soils/thermophilic-composting-hot/)
- [Composting Bacteria, UF/IFAS](https://sfyl.ifas.ufl.edu/sarasota/natural-resources/waste-reduction/composting/what-is-composting/meet-your-composting-team/composting-bacteria/)
